_comment: Technique walkthroughs. content_status='authored' = reviewed, safe to present as guidance; 'placeholder' = scaffold that still needs real authoring (and is labeled as such in the UI). Technique cues are NOT food-safety verdicts — doneness lives in doneness.json.

techniques.name: Marinate
techniques.definition: Soak food in a seasoned liquid so flavor (and sometimes acid/salt) penetrates the surface before cooking.

stages.look: Food fully coated and submerged in the marinade.
stages.action_cue: Cover and refrigerate for the time the recipe gives. Don't marinate at room temperature.

stages.look: Surface looks slightly cured/glossy; acid may lighten the color of the meat.
stages.action_cue: Shake or wipe off excess before cooking so it sears instead of steaming. Reserve marinade only if you'll cook it.

techniques.name: Brown the meat
techniques.definition: Cook the surface of meat in hot fat until it develops a deep brown crust (the Maillard reaction). This is for flavor — it does NOT mean the meat is cooked through.

stages.look: Oil shimmers and the surface is dry; the pan is hot.
stages.sound: A loud, immediate sizzle when the meat hits the pan.
stages.action_cue: Add meat in a single layer with space between pieces; don't crowd the pan or it steams.

stages.look: Edges turn from pink/grey to brown; a crust forms underneath.
stages.action_cue: Leave it undisturbed until it releases easily, then turn. Brown in batches if needed.
Browning is a flavor step, not a doneness step — set pieces aside and finish cooking per the recipe, then check doneness.

techniques.name: Thicken a sauce
techniques.definition: Use a starch slurry (flour or cornstarch mixed with cold liquid) to turn a thin sauce glossy and coating.

stages.look: Starch fully dissolved in cold liquid — no lumps — before it goes in.
stages.action_cue: Always mix the starch into COLD liquid first; adding dry starch to hot liquid makes lumps.

stages.look: Sauce dulls from watery to slightly cloudy, then turns glossy.
stages.action_cue: Stir constantly and let it reach a simmer for a minute or two — starch only thickens once it's heated through.

stages.look: Sauce coats the back of a spoon; a finger swiped through leaves a clear line.
stages.action_cue: Stop here. Over-boiling cornstarch can thin it back out.

techniques.name: Simmer
techniques.definition: Cook in liquid just below a boil — small bubbles, gentle movement — to cook through gently without toughening.

stages.look: Small bubbles rise occasionally; the surface barely moves (not a rolling boil).
stages.action_cue: Adjust heat to keep it at a bare simmer; cover or uncover per the recipe. (Needs photo-style cue authoring.)
